themes.heading: Sicherheitsstufe
themes.text: country-security"> Stand 19.07.2026 (Unverändert gültig seit: 24.06.2026) Sicherheitsstufe Sicherheitsstufe 2 Die Sicherheitslage kann sich jederzeit verändern und entwickeln. Daher empfehlen wir Ihnen: Nützen Sie unsere Auslandsservice-App sowie die kostenlose Reiseregistrierung! Informieren Sie sich über die Sicherheitslage vor Ort und überprüfen Sie diese regelmäßig! Aktuelle Hinweise Nach dem zweiten Wahlgang der Präsidentschaftswahlen in Peru am 7. Juni 2026 ist die Stimmenauszählung weiterhin nicht abgeschlossen. Vor diesem Hintergrund sind Protestaktionen und Demonstrationen verschiedener gesellschaftlicher Gruppen jederzeit möglich. Für die Metropolregion Lima, die angrenzende Region Callao sowie die Städte Trujillo und Virú in der Region La Libertad und die Städte Tumbes und Zarumilla in der Region Tumbes besteht weiterhin ein Ausnahmezustand. Darüber hinaus hat die peruanische Regierung den Ausnahmezustand in mehreren weiteren Landesteilen um 60 Tage verlängert. In den Distrikten Palca, Tacna, La Yarada-Los Palos und Tarata in der Region Tacna dient diese Maßnahme der Bekämpfung von Kriminalität und Gewalt. Zudem gilt seit dem 25. Februar 2026 ein Ausnahmezustand in insgesamt 178 Distrikten der Regionen Amazonas, Áncash, Apurímac, Arequipa, Ayacucho, Cajamarca, Huancavelica, Huánuco, Ica, Junín, La Libertad, Lambayeque, Lima, Loreto, Moquegua, Pasco, Piura, San Martín, Tacna, Tumbes

themes.heading: Aktuelle Hinweise
themes.text: Nach dem zweiten Wahlgang der Präsidentschaftswahlen in Peru am 7. Juni 2026 ist die Stimmenauszählung weiterhin nicht abgeschlossen. Vor diesem Hintergrund sind Protestaktionen und Demonstrationen verschiedener gesellschaftlicher Gruppen jederzeit möglich. Für die Metropolregion Lima, die angrenzende Region Callao sowie die Städte Trujillo und Virú in der Region La Libertad und die Städte Tumbes und Zarumilla in der Region Tumbes besteht weiterhin ein Ausnahmezustand. Darüber hinaus hat die peruanische Regierung den Ausnahmezustand in mehreren weiteren Landesteilen um 60 Tage verlängert. In den Distrikten Palca, Tacna, La Yarada-Los Palos und Tarata in der Region Tacna dient diese Maßnahme der Bekämpfung von Kriminalität und Gewalt. Zudem gilt seit dem 25. Februar 2026 ein Ausnahmezustand in insgesamt 178 Distrikten der Regionen Amazonas, Áncash, Apurímac, Arequipa, Ayacucho, Cajamarca, Huancavelica, Huánuco, Ica, Junín, La Libertad, Lambayeque, Lima, Loreto, Moquegua, Pasco, Piura, San Martín, Tacna, Tumbes und Ucayali. Hintergrund sind die Auswirkungen starker Regenfälle und die damit verbundenen Risiken. Während des Ausnahmezustands können bestimmte verfassungsmäßige Rechte, darunter die Unverletzlichkeit der Wohnung, die Bewegungsfreiheit, die Versammlungsfreiheit sowie die persönliche Freiheit und Sicherheit, eingeschränkt werden. Die Aufrechterhaltung der öffentlichen Ordnung erfolgt durch die Nationalpolizei mit Unterstützung der Streitkräfte. Reisenden wird empfohlen, die aktuelle Sicherheitslage über peruanische Medien, beispielsweise die Informationskanäle der peruanischen Polizei, aufmerksam zu verfolgen, umsichtig zu handeln und unnötige Risiken zu vermeiden. Sicherheitsempfehlungen Verfolgen Sie die Sicherheitslage in den peruanischen Medien, z.B. über den Kanal der peruanischen Polizei, bleiben Sie wachsam und gehen Sie keine unnötigen Risiken ein. Informieren Sie sich regelmäßig über die aktuelle Lage bei den regionalen Büros der peruanischen Touristeninformation (iPeru), in lokalen Medien sowie bei INDECI. Halten Sie sich von großen Menschenansammlungen und Demonstrationen fern. Folgen Sie den Anweisungen der peruanischen Behörden. Planen Sie hinreichend Zeit ein und machen Sie ggf. alternative Reisepläne. Versuchen Sie nicht, Blockaden zu überwinden. Dies kann zu aggressiven Reaktionen der Demonstranten führen. ​​​​​​​Bei Reisen in Berg- und Schluchtenregionen ist besondere Vorsicht geboten, da es auch ohne vorherige starke Niederschläge lokal zu plötzlichen Sturzfluten kommen kann. Versuchen Sie nicht, auf eigene Faust durch überflutete Straßen oder Flussläufe zu gehen oder zu fahren.

themes.heading: Sicherheit & Kriminalität
themes.text: Sicherheitsrisiko (Sicherheitsstufe 2) Die politische Situation ist angespannt, so dass Proteste mit Ausschreitungen in verschiedenen Landesteilen schnell aufflackern könnten. Insbesondere in den südlichen Bergregionen (Puno, Titicacasee, Ayacucho, Cusco) ist die politisch-soziale Lage extrem fragil. Bei Reisen nach Peru ist es höchst angeraten, die lokalen Entwicklungen im jeweiligen Reisegebiet laufend genau zu verfolgen. Ausreichende Kenntnis der Landessprache (Spanisch) ist empfehlenswert Demonstrationen und Menschenansammlungen und die Elendsviertel in Großstädten sind grundsätzlich zu meiden. Hohe Kriminalität (Diebstahl, Raubüberfälle, Entführungen) vor allem in Lima, Cusco, Trujillo, Arequipa, Puno und Huaraz, im öffentlichen Nahverkehr, an Busterminals, in Einkaufsstraßen und auf Märkten. Besondere Vorsicht gilt für Frauen im Hinblick auf sexuelle Übergriffe. Vor allem nachts ist äußerste Vorsicht geboten, leisten Sie bei Überfällen keine Gegenwehr! In den folgenden Regionen ist der Ausnahmezustand ausgerufen: Trujillo, Pataz und Virú (La Libertad), Zarumilla (Tumbes), Putumayo und Mariscal Ramón Castilla (Loreto) und in einzelnen Bezirken und Bevölkerungszentren in den Regionen Ayacucho, Huancavelica, Cusco und Junín. Die peruanische Nationalpolizei sorgt mit Unterstützung der Streitkräfte für die Kontrolle der inneren Ordnung. Soziale Unruhen, Streiks und Demonstrationen führen immer wieder zu teilweise gewaltsamen Auseinandersetzungen und Straßenblockaden, die auch zu Behinderungen im Reiseverkehr führen können. Unter "Alerta de estado de vía" (https://gis.sutran.gob.pe/alerta_sutran/) kann die aktuelle Lage auf den Straßen Perus tagesaktuell beobachtet werden. Es werden derzeit vermehrt Fälle verzeichnet, bei denen Reisenden das Boarding des Fluges verweigert wird, wenn das Flugticket nicht vom Reisenden selbst gekauft wurde. Informieren Sie sich vor der Reise bei der jeweiligen Fluggesellschaft, welche Vorschriften hierfür gelten. Notrufnummern: Allgemeine Notrufnummer: 105 Ambulanz: +51 1 416 67 77, Alerta Medical Feuerwehr: 116 Polizei: 105 Touristenpolizei: + 51 1 460 10 60 Zivilschutz: 115 Relevante Krisennotfallnummern: Gesundheitliche Notfälle: 117 Autobahnpolizei: 110 Rotes Kreuz: +51 1 266 04 81

themes.heading: Einreise & Ausreise
themes.text: Visumpflicht: Nein (visumfrei für 90 Tage) Visum erhältlich: Für Aufenthalte über 90 Tage muss ein Visum bei der peruanischen Botschaft in Wien beantragt werden. Eine Verlängerung des visumfreien Aufenthaltes ist nur in besonderen Fällen direkt bei der Migrationsbehörde in Lima möglich. Es wird empfohlen, die bei der Einreise genehmigte Aufenthaltsdauer zu erfragen, da diese fallweise kürzer als 90 Tage festgelegt werden kann. Die genehmigte Aufenthaltsdauer kann auch über die Webseite der TAM (Tarjeta Andina de Migracion Superintendencia Nacional de Migraciones | Consultas en Línea) abgefragt werden. Reisedokumente: Reisepass. Passgültigkeit: Mindestens 6 Monate bei Einreise. Cremefarbiger Notpass: Wird akzeptiert, 6 Monate Mindestgültigkeit bei Einreise. Opfern von Passdiebstählen wird vermehrt das Boarding von Inlandsflügen verweigert, auch wenn ein Polizeibericht der peruanischen Polizei vorliegt. Dies hat zur Folge, dass Betroffene gezwungen sind, über den Landweg nach Lima zu reisen, um einen Notpass zu beantragen. Sonstiges: Für die Einreise nach Peru ist es notwendig ein Ausreiseticket vorlegen zu können. Der Reisepass bzw. eine Kopie sollten immer mitgeführt werden. Bei Passverlust ist die polizeiliche Anzeige bei der Ausreise vorzuweisen. Ausländer, die über die Grenzen von Peru nach Ecuador einreisen, müssen einen Strafregisterauszug ihres Herkunftslandes oder ihres Wohnsitzlandes für die letzten fünf Jahre vorlegen. Das Dokument muss apostilliert sein. Weiters müssen ab dem 12.05.2025 in Peru wohnhafte Ausländer und Reisende, welche sich länger als 10 Tage in Peru aufgehalten haben, bei Einreise in Ecuador einen Gelbfieberimpfnachweis vorweisen (gilt für peruanische Staatsangehörige generell). Die Impfung muss mindestens 10 Tage vor Reiseantritt durchgeführt worden sein Minderjährige: Für Minderjährige (bis 18 Jahre), die ohne Begleitung der gesetzlichen Vertreter oder mit nur einem gesetzlichen Vertreter verreisen, wird – zusätzlich zum eigenen Reisepass – eine apostillierte Geburtsurkunde sowie eine notariell beglaubigte Einverständniserklärung der Eltern (oder des Elternteils, der nicht mitreist) für die Reise benötigt. Die Einverständniserklärung sollte folgende Informationen enthalten: persönliche Daten der Eltern und des Minderjährigen, Adresse, Kopie des Reisepasses von beiden Elternteilen und des Minderjährigen sowie Reisedaten. Bei unterschiedlichen Nachnamen empfiehlt sich auch die Mitnahme der Heiratsurkunde der Eltern. Es wird empfohlen, sich vor der Reise bei der peruanischen Botschaft in Wien (Autorizacion de viajes de menores de edad) zu informieren sowie auch bei der gewählten Fluglinie, da diese die aktuellen Erfordernisse kennt. Muster für eine Einverständniserklärung finden Sie auf der Seite des ÖAMTC und des ARBÖ. Diebstahl/Verlust des Reisedokuments: Von der Verwendung gestohlener oder verlorener und wieder gefundener Reisedokumente wird abgeraten, auch wenn die Anzeige bei der zuständigen Behörde bereits widerrufen wurde, da Probleme an der Grenze bis zur Einreiseverweigerung nicht ausgeschlossen werden können. Konventions- oder Fremdenpass: Wenden Sie sich bitte an die weiter unten angeführte „ausländische Vertretung in Österreich“.

themes.heading: Einfuhr & Ausfuhr
themes.text: Die Ein- und Ausfuhr von Landes- und Fremdwährung über USD 10.000 muss deklariert werden, die Einfuhr von Bargeld über USD 30.000 ist verboten. Die Mitnahme von Bargeld (Euro und US-Dollar) oder Kreditkarten wird empfohlen. Für Reisen außerhalb Limas empfiehlt sich die Mitnahme von US-Dollar in kleinen Scheinen. Gegenstände für den persönlichen Bedarf und Geschenke im Wert von USD 300 können zollfrei eingeführt werden. Die Ausfuhr von historischen Kunstgegenständen und lebenden oder präparierten exotischen Tieren ist streng verboten. Zollinformationen für Peru in spanischer Sprache und eine App zur Deklarierung von zollpflichtigen Gegenständen finden Sie hier: El equipaje Asistente Aduanero (sunat.gob.pe) Nähere Auskünfte finden Sie auch im Travel Centre der IATA. Die angeführten Mengen und Beträge sind unverbindliche Richtangaben, rechtsverbindliche Informationen kann nur die Vertretungsbehörde dieses Landes erteilen. Bitte beachten Sie bei der Einreise nach Österreich die geltenden Einfuhrbestimmungen.

themes.heading: Gesundheit
themes.text: Die medizinische Betreuung ist außerhalb von Lima unzureichend. Krankenhäuser verlangen bei der Aufnahme eine Kreditkarte oder ein Gelddepot. In Höhenregionen besteht die Gefahr von Höhenkrankheit und in den Urwaldgebieten die Gefahr von Tropenkrankheiten. Für manche Regionen empfiehlt das peruanische Gesundheitsministerium eine Gelbfieberimpfung. In den Armenvierteln der Städte und in entlegenen Landesteilen treten vereinzelte Fälle von Typhus und Cholera auf. DENGUE-Fieberfälle treten bereits in 23 der 24 Regionen Perus auf. Obwohl das epidemiologische Verhalten den Erwartungen entspricht, ruft das Gesundheitsministerium zur Vorsicht auf und empfiehlt die Verwendung von Mückenschutzmittel, langärmeliger Kleidung und langen Hosen. Da diese Virusinfektion nicht ansteckend ist, gibt es in Peru keine Einschränkungen in Bezug auf die persönliche Bewegungsfreiheit. Achten Sie auf Insektenschutz zur Vermeidung von Denguefieber (Amazonasgebiet, Madre de Dios aber auch in Teilen des Küstengebietes und Lima/Callao) und anderen Tropenkrankheiten! Ausführliche Informationen zu gängigen Infektionskrankheiten auf Reisen erhalten Sie auch beim Öffentlichen Gesundheitsportal Österreichs bzw. bei den tropenmedizinischen Instituten. Es besteht kein Sozialversicherungsabkommen mit Österreich. Es wird darauf hingewiesen, dass die Kosten einer allfälligen Krankenhausbehandlung erheblich sein können. Der Abschluss einer alle Risiken abdeckenden Reiseversicherung wird daher vor Reiseantritt dringend empfohlen. Die Mitnahme einer Reiseapotheke, die nicht nur regelmäßig benötigte Arzneimittel, sondern auch Medikamente für gängige Reiseerkrankungen beinhaltet, wird empfohlen. Wer auf bestimmte Medikamente angewiesen ist, sollte einen ausreichenden Vorrat und einen Nachweis über die ärztliche Verschreibung mitnehmen, auf der Homepage des Sozialministeriums finden Sie nähere Informationen zur Mitnahme von Medikamenten ins Ausland. Es wird empfohlen, rechtzeitig vor Reisebeginn den Hausarzt oder eine andere geeignete Einrichtung zu kontaktieren, um sich über eventuell erforderliche Reiseimpfungen zu erkundigen.

themes.heading: Infrastruktur
themes.text: Öffentliche Verkehrsverbindungen: Flugverbindungen und Reisebusse, im Amazonasgebiet auch Boote. Nicht gut ausgebautes Straßennetz, vor allem nicht asphaltierte Nebenstraßen sind unsicher. Es kommt immer wieder zu schweren Verkehrsunfällen, nachts häufig auch zu Überfällen. Verwenden Sie nur registrierte Taxis bzw. für Überlandfahrten renommierte Verkehrsunternehmen . Der österreichische Führerschein kann bis zu 6 Monate nach Einreise verwendet werden. Verkehrsstrafen sind nur beim Servicio de Administración Tributaria (derzeit nur in Peru aufrufbar) in Lima und in den Filialen der Nationalbank in den Provinzen zu bezahlen. Von November bis Mai gibt es in den Bergregionen Verkehrsbehinderungen durch Muren und Überschwemmungen. Mehr Informationen zur Straßenverkehrsordnung finden Sie in der Länderdatenbank des ÖAMTC.

themes.heading: Umwelt
themes.text: Gemäßigtes Klima in der Küstenregion, heiß von Dezember bis März, Winternebel von Mai bis November mit sehr hoher Luftfeuchtigkeit während des ganzen Jahres. In der Andenregion entsprechend der Höhenlage variables Klima, trocken von Mai bis Oktober, starke Regenfälle von November bis April. In der Amazonasregion ganzjährig heißes, tropisches Klima mit hoher Luftfeuchtigkeit. Peru liegt in einer seismisch aktiven Zone, es kann jederzeit zu Erdbeben kommen. Weitere Informationen unter Regionale Hinweise, Nationales US-Geologieinstitut, Österreichischer Zivilschutzverband (mit Erdbebenschutz-Ratgeber).

themes.heading: Besondere Bestimmungen
themes.text: Rauschgiftdelikte werden mit langjährigen Haftstrafen geahndet. Bergsteigern wird empfohlen, sich vor einer Bergtour bei der örtlichen Bergpolizei und bei der Botschaft zu registrieren und Kontakt mit privaten Rettungsunternehmen z.B. SARA Peru herzustellen. Im Falle einer Bergung ist von staatlichen Stellen kaum mit Hilfe zu rechnen. Das Schutzalter für sexuelle Handlungen kann gegenüber den in Österreich geltenden Bestimmungen um einige Jahre höher sein oder sogar über dem Erwachsenenalter von 18 Jahren liegen. Es können jedoch auch (beispielsweise in einzelnen Provinzen oder Regionen) unterschiedliche Bestimmungen zur Anwendung kommen.

themes.heading: 기본정보 (basisinformatie)
themes.text: ㅇ 국명 : 페루 공화국 (República del Perú/Republic of Peru) ㅇ 위치 : 남아메리카 대륙 (북위 0°01‘48”, 남위 18°21’03”, 서경 68°39‘27“, 서경 81°19‘34.5“ 사이에 위치) - 태평양 연안국가로 에콰도르, 콜롬비아, 브라질, 볼리비아, 칠레 등 5개국과 국경을 접함 ㅇ 면적 : 1,285,215㎢ (한반도의 약 6배, 세계 20위, 남미 3위) - 해안지대 10%, 안데스산맥 산악지대 27%, 아마존 등 정글지대 63% 분포 ㅇ 기후 : 위도상으로 열대와 아열대권으로 구분. 지리적으로 해안지대 (la costa, 사막성 온난 다습), 산악지대(la sierra, 우기와 건기로 구분), 산림지대(la selva, 열대성 기후) 등 크게 3가지 기후대로 분류함 [리마 지역 기후] - 연평균 기온 22℃ (여름최고 30℃, 겨울최저 11℃) - 편의상 여름(12~5월) 및 겨울(6월-11월)로 구분 - 겨울 6개월(6월~11월)간 짙은 안개에 뒤덮여 햇빛을 볼 수 없으며, 습도가 높음 ㅇ 수도 : 리마 ㅇ 주요 도시 : 아레끼빠 (Arequipa : 남부 고산도시, 인구 86만), 뜨루히요(Trujillo : 북부 해안도시, 인구 78만), 이끼또스 (Iquitos : 정글지역, 인구 43만), 꾸스꼬(Cusco : 잉카제국 수도, 인구 42만) ㅇ 인종 : 인디오(원주민) 45%, 메스티소(혼혈계) 37%, 백인 15%, 흑인 및 동양인 3% - 중국계 약 1백만, 일본계 약 10만 등 아시아계가 약 4~5% 라는 비공식 통계가 있음 ㅇ 언어 : 스페인어(84%가 모국어로 사용), 께추아어(Quechua, 13%가 모국어로 사용), 아이마라어(Aymara, 1.7%가 모국어로 사용) 등 3개 공식 언어 외에 아샤닌까어(Ashaninka) 등 다양한 소수 민족 언어를 일부가 사용 ㅇ 종교 : 가톨릭교 81.3%, 기독교 12.5%, 무교 및 기타 6.2%

themes.heading: 페루 입국전
themes.text: 1. 여행자 보험 가입, 여권 유효기간 확인, 현지 안전 정보 파악 ㅇ (중요) 사건 사고 및 병원 입원시에 대비해 당지 방문 전에 여행자 보험을 필수로 가입 하기를 권고 - 수도 리마가 아닌 지방에서 부상을 당할 경우, 현지 의료기관이 필요한 장비를 갖추고 있지 않아 정확한 진단이 어려울 수 있으며, 환자 이송(수송기 별도 임차 필요, 이용객 부담) 또한 쉽지 않은 상황이 발생할 수 있는바, 여행자 보험 가입 요망 ㅇ 페루 입국시 여권 유효기간 6개월 이상 필요 - 페루 정부(이민청)는 외국인이 페루 입국시 유효기간이 6개월 이상인 여권을 소지할 것을 안내하고 있고, 실제로 동 규정을 준수하지 않아 입국에 어려움을 겪는 사례가 종종 발생하고 있음. 유효기간이 충분하지 않은 여권을 소지할 경우 이전 여행지로 돌아가야 하는 경우가 발생할 수 있으니, 여행 전에 여권 유효기간을 반드시 확인. ㅇ 외교부 해외안전여행정보, 주페루대사관 홈페이지-안전여행정보 등에서 당지 안전여행 정보 파악 및 숙지 필요 - www.0404.go.kr, overseas.mofa.go.kr/pe-ko/index.do - ▴국가별 최신 안전정보 실시간 푸시 알림, ▴여행일정 및 비상연락처 등록, ▴가족 또는 지인에게 위치정보 전송 기능 사건ㆍ사고 현황 [최근 치안 동향] ㅇ 페루내 치안 불안 요소가 가중되고 치안이 악화될 가능성이 높은 만큼 개인 신변 안전에 상시 유의 - 코로나 19 사태와 베네수엘라 정치 상황 악화 이후 지속 유입된 베네수엘라 이주민으로 인해 전국적으로 범죄 발생 건수가 증가하고 있는 것으로 나타남. - 수도 리마 도심 상권에서 베네수엘라 조직 범죄단과 페루 조직간 불법적 갈취를 둘러싼 갈등이 심화되고 있어, 치안이 우려 되는 상황임. 북부 지역 국경의 경우, 에콰도르 정세 불안으로 불법 이민자들이 다수 있는 것으로 파악되며, 이로 인해 불안 요소가 상존하는 것으로 분석됨. ㅇ 2024.9월 우리 국민 납치 사건이 발생한바, 페루 방문 및 체류시 각별히 주의 - 치안 불안 지역을 홀로 이동하지 않도록 유의하고, 특히 야간에 이동할 경우에는 가능한 한 지인과 동행하여 이동하고 가까운 거리라도 택시를 이용할 것 - 납치범을 자극하지 말고 몸값 요구를 위한 서한이나 녹음을 요청할 때는 응하고, 납치가 되어 인질이 된 경우, 자제력을 잃지 말고 납치범과 대화를 지속하여 우호적인 관계를 형성 ㅇ 미 국무부 여행경보 2단계 조정 및 유지 - 미 국무부, 2023.11.15.(수) 현재 페루에 대한 여행경보(Travel Advisory)를 2단계(Exercise increased caution, 강화된 사전 주의)로 유지중(①페루내에서 절도, 차량 탈취, 강도, 폭행 등의 범죄가 주간 또는 군중 밀집도와 상관없이 만연하고 있으며, 조직범죄 집단이 수도 리마 외각에서 도로를 점거하고 강도 행위를 벌이고 있음. ②미국인을 포함한 다수의 여행객들이 페루 전통의식(Ayahuasca, Kambo 등)에 참여하여 환각 성분이 들어간 물질을 섭취한 후, 성폭행, 강도, 건강 악화, 사망에 이르기도 하므로 각별한 주의를 요함. ③미국 정부 관계자들은 치안 문제로 페루 내에서 자유롭게 여행하는데 어려움이 있음. ④▲콜롬비아-페루 국경 지역(Loreto주), , ▲푸노(Puno)주, Apurimac주, ▲브라엠(Vraem)* 지역은 4단계(Do not travel)로 지정되어 계속 유지 되고 있음. * 브라엠(VRAEM) 지역은 페루 중부 5개 주(쿠스코, 아푸리막, 아야쿠쵸 우안카벨리카, 후닌) 인근 열대우림 지역으로 페루 최대, 전 세계 2대 코카 생산지임. 넓은 지형, 열대우림, 이동 가능한 강 등 지리적 이유로 마약운반책들의 주요 활동 지역임. 이러한 상황에서 페루 정부는 브라엠 지역에 비상사태를 선포했으며 계속 적용시기를 연장해오고 있음. (60일 단위로 지속 연장해오고 있는바, 연장 가능성 높음)

themes.heading: 사건ㆍ사고의 유형
themes.text: [주요 사건 사고 동향 및 예방법] □ 시내 : 강도, 소매치기 및 택시 관련 범죄 주의, 교통사고 유의 ㅇ 길에서 잡은 택시를 타고 이동 중, 현지 기사가 강도로 돌변하여 피해를 입은 사례 - 현지 사정에 어두운 외국인 및 여행객은 가장 쉬운 범죄 표적으로, 총기 등 흉기로 위협하거나, 2-3인조가 한패가 되어 강탈하는 사례 빈번 - 시내 이동 시에는 대중교통 수단을 피하고, 가급적 택시 어플리케이션(Beat, Uber 등)을 이용하는 것이 편리(흥정 불필요)하고, 상대적으로 안전함 ㅇ 도보 이용시, 차량 사고 및 오토바이 소매치기 피해 사례 - 리마, 지방 대도시의 시내 교통은 상시 혼잡하고 위험하여, 도보 이동 시 매우 주의를 요함. 특히, 차도를 건널 때에는 필히 횡단보도를 이용하되, 녹색 신호에도 전방 및 좌우를 잘 살펴야 함 - 쇼핑백, 가방 등을 소지하고 이동시에 오토바이를 이용한 소매치기 다수 발생. 큰 길에서 도보 이동시, 차량 진행 방향과 반대 방향으로 걸어가고 개인 소지품 관리에 유의할 것 ㅇ 식당에서 식사 중, 의자 등받이에 걸어 놓거나 의자 밑에 놓아둔 가방 도난 사례 - 한국에서는 카페, 식당 등 공공장소에 둔 물건을 도난, 분실하는 사례가 상대적으로 적어 해외여행시 개인 소지품 관리에 소홀해 질 수 있으나, 이는 매우 손쉬운 표적이 되고 있으므로 상시 주의가 필요함 (가방은 품 안에 보관 요망) ㅇ 카드를 도난당하여, 카드 결제 및 현금이 인출되는 피해 발생 - 강도, 소매치기 당한 가방이나 지갑 속에 있던 카드로 인해 2차 피해가 발생하고 있음. 이를 예방하기 위해 해외여행 전에 카드 결제, 인출 한도를 조정하는 것을 권장함 ㅇ 개인 또는 간이 환전소에서 환전 시, 위조지폐 포함 및 무장 강도에 표적이 된 사례 - 노상에서 환전 등 금전 거래를 하는 것은 즉시 범죄의 표적이 될 수 있는 매우 위험한 행동으로 절대 삼가야 함. 아울러, 환전 사기(위폐)를 피하기 위해서는 은행 또는 공인된 환전소를 이용하는 것을 권장함 ㅇ 이동 중이던 차량이 정차한 사이, 강도가 총기를 이용하여 위협 후 귀중품을 강탈해 간 사례 - ▲차량 정차시, 창문 올리기, ▲차내 핸드폰 사용 자제, ▲차내 캐리어 및 소지품 보관시, 외부에서 눈에 띄지 않는 곳에 보관, ▲인적이 드문 야간 및 주말 오전 이동 자제 □ 시외 여행 중 : 안전 지침 숙지, 도난 빈번 발생, 전염병 주의 ㅇ 페루는 고속도로 교통사고가 빈번히 발생하는 바, 기차, 버스 등을 통한 페루내 ▴육로 이동시 안전 벨트 착용, ▴차량 이동시 반드시 착석, ▴비상사태시 탈출할 수 있는 비상구 사전 확보 및 탈출 방법 구상 필요 ㅇ 장거리 운행버스 짐칸 또는 선반에 두었던 소지품/짐을 도난당한 사례 - 버스 등 여객회사에 수화물을 맡길 때에는 반드시 수화물표를 받아두어야하고, 귀중품등은 직접 소지하는 등 만약에 있을 분실사고에 대비 필요 ㅇ 길링-바레 증후군, 말라리아, 원숭이두창, 코로나19 등에 유의, ▴개인 위생 철저, ▴발병지 방문 자제, ▴개인 면역 체계 강화 - (길링-바레 증후군) 따뜻한 물로 손 씻기, 위생적이지 않은 식당이나 장소에서 음식물 섭취 지양(노점상 등), 과일 및 채소류는 섭취 전 반드시 세척, (말라리아) 외출시 긴옷 착용 및 모기 기피제 사용, 취침시 가급적 창문을 닫고, 모기장 활용, 말라리아 위험국가 여행 전 필요시 병원에 내방하여 의사와 상담후 예방약 복용, 말라리아 증상(오한, 발열 등)발현 즉시 가까운 병원 내방 □ 산악지역 여행 중 : 낙마, 교통사고 발생, 가족과의 연락 두절, 고산증 ㅇ 정글 트레킹 중 낙마하여 교통사고를 당한 사례 - 자전거 등을 이용하여 트레킹을 하는 여행 상품에 참여시, ▴여행자 보험 가입 필수, ▴안전 장비 착용, ▴교통 사고 유의, ▴폭우시 참여 자제 등 안전에 유의 ㅇ 고산 지역 여행시 유의 사항(중요) - 저지대에서 고지대로 적응과정 없이 올라갈 경우, 저기압 및 저산소 상태에 신체가 적응하지 못하는 고산증이 발생할 수 있고, 고도와 개인의 민감도 등에 따라 ▴두통, ▴구토, ▴어지러움, ▴피로, ▴숨가쁨, ▴식욕감퇴, ▴수면장애 등의 증상 유발 - ▴여유있는 계획으로 무리한 일정 강행을 피할 것, ▴충분한 수분 섭취, ▴담배, 술, 신경안정제, 수면제 등 호흡을 약화시키는 물질은 피하고, 고지방과 고단백질의 섭취 자제(소식 필요), ▴필요시 예방약 복용 ㅇ 가족 및 지인과 장시간 연락이 되지 않는 상황에 처하게 될 경우 - ▲정글 투어, ▲산악 트레킹, ▲육로를 통한 국경 이동 등 페루내 일부 지역은 전력 혹은 통신망이 원활하지 않아 장시간 연락이 되지 않을 가능성이 크고 이를 악용한 보이스피싱 시도 - 여행 전에 가족 혹은 가까운 지인에게 이동 경로, 연락 가능 번호, 숙소 등에 대한 정보 공유 - 다만, SNS, 카카오톡 프로필 등 불특정 다수가 볼 수 있는 공간은 종종 보이스피싱 등에 악용될 사례가 있으니 노출 자제 □ 사막지역 여행 중 : 각종 액티비티 안전사고 ㅇ 샌드보드, 사륜오토바이(ATV) 등 액티비티를 즐기던 중, ▴이용자 간 충돌, ▴전방 주시 태만, ▴차량 전복 등으로 부상을 입은 사례 (지속 발생) - 이카(Ica) 및 우아카치나(Huacachina) 지역 등에서 여행 상품에 참여시, ▴안전 장비 착용, ▴무리한 사진 촬영 자제 등 개인 신변 안전에 특별히 유의 요망

themes.heading: 예방법
themes.text: ㅇ 낯선 사람 접근시, 침착하게 그 상황을 피할 것(길을 건너거나 상가나 마트 등 다수가 운집한 곳으로 진입) ㅇ 강도가 흉기 등을 활용하여 위협할 경우, 저항하지 말고 원하는 물품 전달할 것 ㅇ 항상 주위를 경계하고 현재 위치를 파악할 것 ㅇ 차량 탑승시, 귀중품이나 가방 등은 트렁크나 밖에서 보이지 않는 곳에 보관할 것 ㅇ 외출시 최소한의 물품만 휴대할 것(휴대폰, 신분증, 소정의 현금, 신용카드 등) ㅇ 수상한 차량 경계할 것(의심되는 차량이 접근할 경우 우회하거나 경로를 바꿀 것) ㅇ 안전한 곳에 주차하고 창문을 닫을 것 ㅇ 거주지의 창문과 문을 항상 닫고, 필요시 경보 장치를 활용할 것 ㅇ 신종 사기 수법(보이스피싱, 가정방문 등)에 유의할 것 ㅇ 택시 탑승시, 어플리케이션 혹은 콜택시를 활용할 것(이동시 경로를 휴대폰으로 확인할 것) ㅇ 신호 대기 및 정차시, 낯선 사람이 접근하는 것 경계할 것 ㅇ 길이나 차량, 야간에 휴대폰 사용 자제 ㅇ 어두운 곳이나 한적한 곳 외출 자제 ㅇ 불필요한 야간 외출 자제, 우범지역 및 대규모 인원 운집지역 방문 자제, 신분증 지참

themes.heading: 출입국 (in- en uitreis)
themes.text: 무비자 입국 가능 여부(1회 입국시 최대 연속 체류 가능 일자) ㅇ 외교관 여권 소지자 : O, (외교목적: 임무기간) (협정) [참고자료] ▪ 페루 이민법 규정(Artículo 96 Titulo IV Calidad Migratoria - DS 1350) ㅇ 관용여권 소지자 : O, (외교목적: 임무기간) (협정) [참고자료] ▪ 페루 이민법 규정(Artículo 96 Titulo IV Calidad Migratoria - DS 1350) ㅇ 일반여권 소지자(복수와 단수를 구분할 경우 별도 표기 필요) : O, (최대 90일의 기간 부여) (협정) ㅇ 도착사증 발급 여부 : 도착사증 미발급 입국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 여행당 $1,000 이내 또는 연간 총 $3,000 이내 가치에 해당하는 소지품은 12% 세금 지불, 그 이상은 일반 수입 관세 지불/현금 $10,000 이상 소지 시 신고, 최대 $30,000까지 반입 가능 ㅇ 식품의 경우 유관기관(SENASA) 사전신고 및 검수 필수, 페루 입국 전 반입불허품목 및 신고 필수 품목확인 필요 [통관 규정 관련 참고자료] - https://www.sunat.gob.pe/orientacionaduanera/mercanciasrestringidas/listas/listaMercanciaProhibida-Importa.pdf - https://www.gob.pe/15286-bienes-y-articulos-no-obligados-a-declarar-y-que-puedes-ingresar-libres-de-impuestos-al-peru - http://www.aduanet.gob.pe/orientacAduana/equipaje/controlEquipajes.htm 출국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 허가된 기간 이상 체류할 경우 공항 내 이민청(Migraciones)에서 불법체류 기간에 해당하는 금액의 벌금 지불 후 출국, 일반적으로 이민청 출국 심사관이 벌금 고지 및 안내(하루당 5.15솔, 2024.1월 기준) ㅇ 육로(자차, 렌터카 등)로 출국 시 관련 규정 확인 필요 [참고자료] - http://www.aduanet.gob.pe/orientacAduana/equipaje/controlEquipajes.htm ㅇ 미성년자의 단독 출국 또는 부모 중 한명과의 동반 출국의 경우 공증사무소에서부모동의서(Authorizacion de Viaje)를 소지하여야 페루 출국 가능 참고자료 ㅇ 해당 국가의 주한공관홈페이지 ㅇ 해당국가의 한국 대사관 홈페이지 - 국문: https://overseas.mofa.go.kr/pe-ko/index.do - 서문: https://overseas.mofa.go.kr/pe-es/index.do ㅇ 기타 참고자료 - 페루 외교부 공식 사이트 : https://www.gob.pe/rree - 페루 이민청 공식 사이트 : https://www.gob.pe/migraciones [페루 외교부 공식 SNS 계정] - 공식 X 계정 (기존 트위터) https://twitter.com/CancilleriaPeru - 공식 페이스북 계정 https://www.facebook.com/CancilleriaPeru/?locale=es_LA 기타 참고자료는 각 셀에 첨부된 링크 참조

themes.heading: 일반문화
themes.text: ㅇ 남녀간 뺨을 부딪히는 인사관습(남미식)이 있으나, 이를 따라하지 않고 악수만 하여도 무방합니다. ㅇ 페루인은 비교적 개방된 편이고 사교성이 높아 관광객들에게 여러 가지 질문하기를 좋아합니다. 또한 한국에 대한 관심이 부쩍 높아진 편이라서 한국에 대한 이미지가 좋습니다

themes.heading: 종교관련
themes.text: ㅇ 페루 국교는 카톨릭이나, 종교의 자유가 보장되어 있으며, 종교에 관련한 특별한 박해나 위해사고는 거의 발생하지 않고 있습니다.

themes.heading: 팁 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 영수증에 봉사료가 포함이 안되어 있는 경우는 5~10%의 팁을 주는 것이 일반적입니다. - 메뉴판을 보시면 봉사료가 포함되어 있다는 문구가 있으니, 주의깊게 살펴보기 바랍니다.

themes.heading: 주요 교통 법규 및 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 자동차 차선과 운전석 위치는 한국과 동일하며, 신호체계도 한국과 유사 ㅇ (운전시) 좌·우회전 시 횡단보도가 있을 경우 보행자 횡단 신호도 같이 켜지므로 도로에 진입 시 보행자들이 건너는지 최대한 주의하여 살펴본 뒤 진입해야 함. 횡단보도가 없다고 해도, 무단횡단이 다대하여 운전시 항상 유의하여야 함.차선 변경 시 방향지시등을 켜지 않고 바로 들어오며, 교차로, 회전차로에서 꼬리물기가 심함 ㅇ (보행시) 횡단보도 보행자 신호가 차량 좌·우회전 신호와 함께 들어옴. 페루는 보행자보다 운전자가 우선시 되는 경향이 강하므로, 보행자 신호가 들어왔다고 해도 바로 건너지 말고 차량 운행 여부를 확인 후 건너야 함 ㅇ (택시 탑승시) 도로에서 택시를 탈 경우, 택시 미터기를 사용하지 않기에 탑승 전 택시기사에게 목적지를 말하고 택시비를 흥정한 뒤 탑승해야 함. 다만, 외국인 관광객으로 보이고 언어가 통하지 않을 경우 요금을 몇배로 올려 받거나, 목적지가 아닌 다른 지역으로 데려가 강도 등을 하는 경우가 많음. 택시를 타려고 하는 경우, 택시서비스 어플리케이션(Uber, Beat, Didi, In-Drive)을 사용하는 것을 추천함

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 사례
themes.text: ㅇ 택시나 차량 내부에서 핸드폰이나 귀중품을 소지 하거나 창문이 열려있는 경우 그 틈사이로 손이 들어와 물품을 가지고 달아나기도 함. 치안이 좋지 않은 지역에서는 창문을 올리고 있어도 차량 창문을 깨고 핸드폰, 지갑, 귀중품 등을 훔쳐서 달아나는 경우도 있어 항상 유의해야 함 ㅇ 도로에서 택시를 잡아 가격 흥정 후 이동하던 중, 택시 기사가 언어가 잘 통하지 않는 점을 악용하여 목적지가 아닌 다른 지역으로 운행함. 그 곳에서 다른 일행을 태워 핸드폰, 지갑 등의 귀중품을 탈취한 뒤 외곽지역에 내려두고 사라짐 ㅇ 택시를 타고 가던 중 정지신호를 받고 택시가 서 있는 틈에 오토바이가 다가와 택시 창문을 깨고 택시 기사 및 한국인 승객의 핸드폰, 지갑, 여권 등을 훔쳐 달아남

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 발생시 대처방법
themes.text: ㅇ 사건사고 발생 즉시 가장 가까운 경찰서에 신고함과 동시에 대사관 긴급연락처로 사건사고 상황을 알려야 함. 경찰서에서 경찰리포트를 작성하여 발급받아야 하며, 여권 분실시는 대사관에서 여권 발급 절차를 진행해야 함 ㅇ 경찰 및 응급 신고번호 : 105 ㅇ 영사콜센터에서 스페인어 3자 통역을 지원하므로, 언어적으로 소통이 되지 않을 경우 영사콜센터에 연락하여 통역서비스 요청 - (해외에서 전화시) +822-3210-0404 [운송 수단별 참고 사항] □ 항공 ㅇ 남미의 허브공항이라고 불리는 페루의 공항은 많은 관광객이 방문·경유함. 따라서 국제선 출발 시간이 몰려있는 저녁 시간에는 상당히 혼잡하며, 타국에 비해 오버부킹이 많은 편임 ㅇ 공항에 늦게 도착하여 비행기를 탑승하지 못한 경우가 발생하고 있으니, 국제선의 경우 최소한 출발 3시간 전, 국내선의 경우 2시간 전에 도착하여 탑승수속을 하는 것이 좋고, 항공사별로 운영중인 사전 웹체크인 서비스를 이용하는 것도 고려할만한 사항임 □ 철도 ㅇ 페루내 주요 관광 열차는 마추피추 왕복 기차로, 여행을 계획할 경우 사전 구매하는 것을 추천함. 우기철 여행시 해당 열차회사나 여행사에 확인 후 사전 기차표 예약을 하는 것이 필요함 □ 버스 ㅇ 페루 국내 여행을 위해 고속버스와 시내버스가 있으며, 고속버스를 이용하는 경우 정식 등록된 회사를 이용하기를 추천함. 리마의 경우, 종합터미널이 아닌 버스회사별 터미널을 운영하는 경우가 많은 만큼, 출발지를 확인해야 할 필요가 있음. 버스 요금은 회사마다 약간 차이가 있을 수 있음 ㅇ 리마 내에서 시내버스를 탑승하는 경우 소지품 도난의 우려가 있으니 개인 물품 보관에 신경쓰기를 권면함. 리마시는 노후된 차량을 교체작업 중이나 여전히 노후된 차량이 많아 안전 위험에 노출되어 있으니 유의 바람 ㅇ 버스는 콤비(소형승합차), 코스터, 미니버스, 버스로 구분되며, 콤비의 경우 대중교통에 주로 사용되어 교통사고에 많이 노출되고 비인가 운행도 많아 사고 발생시 보험 처리 등의 문제가 있는만큼 가급적 다른 운송 수단을 활용할 것을 추천함 □ 택시 ㅇ 리마에서 택시는 콜택시와 공항택시, 일반택시로 구분됩니다. 콜택시가 상대적으로 안전하며 택시 어플리케이션을 이용하여 택시 탑승하시길 권면함 ㅇ 일반 택시는 노후화된 택시들이 많아 이용에 불편함이 있고, 늦은 시각에는 콜택시 이용을 추천함 □ 차량운전시 ㅇ 차량은 한국과 같이 우측통행이며, 특별한 표시가 없으면 적색신호등에서 우회전이 금지되어 있음. 도로에 일방통행이 많아 운전시 주의가 필요함 ㅇ 일반적으로 교통이 혼잡하고, 특히, 출퇴근 시간에는 신호 없이 차선변경을 하는 경우가 많으므로 각별한 주의가 필요함 ㅇ 무보험 차량이 많고 사고가 발생해도 해결이 어려우므로 접촉사고가 나지 않도록 주의해야 함

themes.heading: 기타 유의사항
themes.text: ㅇ (쿠스코) 자전거를 이용하여 트레킹을 하는 여행 상품에 참여시, 여행자 보험 가입 필수, 안전 장비 착용, 교통사고 유의, 폭우 시 참여 자제 등 특별히 안전에 유의해야 함 ㅇ 국민들이 많이 찾는 와라즈(Huaraz), 쿠스코(Cusco), 아레키파(Arequipa) 등의 관광지는 해발 고도가 3,000m가 넘는 고지대로, 고산병 발병 가능성이 높아 동 지역 방문 전 고산병 예방 약품 구비 및 복용, 발병 시 응급처치 방법 숙지 등 사전 준비를 잘 하여야 함 ㅇ 직접 운전 시, 페루 유명 관광지인 나스카-쿠스코 고속도로 구간이 가장 교통사고가 많이 나는 구간이므로 안전운전에 유의해야 함

themes.heading: 날씨
themes.text: ㅇ 페루의 기후는 준 사막권 해안지대(온난, 다습), 안데스 고산지대(우기, 건기로 구분), 아마존밀림지대(열대성 기후)에 따라 다르나 리마지역 연평균 22℃(하계 최고 30℃, 동계 최저 11℃)이며, 동계(6월~9월) 기간은 짙은 안개로 햇빛을 볼 수 없는 경우가 많습니다. - 쿠스코(해발 3,400m) 지역은 안데스 고산지대에 위치하여 있어 아침·저녁으로 춥고 낮 시간대는 햇빛이 강하므로, 가벼운 외투 또는 추동복을 준비하시는 것이 좋습니다. - 고산지대에 위치해 있는 마추픽추(주요 관광지, 해발 2,400m)는 비교적 따뜻한 기후이나, 우기에 속하는 여름(11월~3월)보다는 겨울(6월~9월)에 방문을 권장합니다. - 티티카카 호수(해발 3,820m)는 1년 내내 아침·저녁으로 서늘하며 낮에는 해가 뜨거우나 겨울에는 서리가 내릴 정도로 춥습니다. - 아마존 지역의 경우, 아열대성 기후로서 습도가 높아 불쾌지수가 높아질 수 있습니다. 또한 여름에는 우기철로 기온이 떨어져 한국의 장마철과 비슷한 날씨를 보입니다. 아마존을 방문할 경우에는 모기를 비롯한 벌레가 많으므로 벌레 퇴치약을 준비하시는 것이 좋습니다. ㅇ 페루는 일반적으로 자외선 노출도가 9~11로 한국보다 강하니, 선글라스나 자외선 차단제를 소지하시는 것이 좋습니다.

themes.heading: 기타
themes.text: ㅇ 의약품을 구입하실 때, 이미 복용하는 약이 있는 경우 처방전이 없더라도 약의 명칭이나 의약품 포장지를 보여주면 구입이 가능합니다. 대체약품을 권하는 경우도 있으니 유의하시기 바랍니다. 또한, 여행 시 비상약(소화제, 설사약, 두통약, 파스 등)을 준비하는 것이 좋습니다. ㅇ 여행자 보험에 가입되어 있는 경우, 보험사에 따라 치료비의 보험처리가 가능하니 관련 영수증과 의사 처방전 등을 보관하시는 것이 좋습니다(가입 보험사 문의). ㅇ 특별 예방 접종을 요구하고 있지 않으나, 아마존 지역을 포함한 열대지역을 방문한다면 황열병 예방접종을 한 Yellow Card를 소지하는 것이 좋습니다. ㅇ 페루와의 사증면제협정으로 인해 관광목적인 경우 최대 90일간은 사증없이 입국이 가능합니다.

regionalBreakdown.excerpt: of the Apurímac, Ene and Mantaro Rivers (VRAEM region) Take additional precautions in these areas. Reconsider your need to travel to the Valley of the Apurimac, Ene and Mantaro Rivers (VRAEM). Cocaine production and trafficking o

themes.heading: Latest update
themes.text: Still current at:19 July 2026 Updated:23 June 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution in Peru. A state of emergency is in effect for Lima and Callao. There will be an increased security presence, with armed forces on the streets in both areas. Political protests, demonstrations and strikes are common in Peru, particularly in the historic centre of Lima. Past demonstrations have turned violent and disrupted public transport services, including transport to and from Machu Picchu. Avoid large gatherings and protests. Check media for updates and follow the instructions of local authorities (see 'Safety'). We advise: Exercise a high degree of caution in Peru overall due to the threat of violent crime. Higher levels apply in some areas. Download Peru Download Americas

themes.heading: Safety
themes.text: A state of emergency is in effect for Lima and Callao. There is an increased security presence, with armed forces on the streets in both areas. Political protests, demonstrations and strikes are common in Peru, particularly in the historic centre of Lima. Past demonstrations have turned violent and disrupted public transport services, including transport to and from Machu Picchu. Check media for updates and follow the instructions of local authorities. Violent crime is common, including in Lima, Cusco and Arequipa. Avoid going out alone, especially at night. Petty crime is common in public areas, hotels and restaurants. Thieves are often well-dressed. Keep your belongings close and valuables out of sight. Street theft of mobile phones has increased. Avoid using your phone at the curbside, as motorbike riders may snatch it. Travellers in Peru can be victims of ‘express kidnappings’ where victims are forced to withdraw money from ATMs. Criminal gangs have also targeted mining operations and tourist vessels on the Amazon River for kidnappings. Virtual kidnapping scams targeting family and friends of travellers also occur. Robberies occur frequently on buses. Never place valuable personal belongings in overhead racks or under your seats, and ensure your possessions, including your passport and other travel documents, are secure at all times. Use only reputable bus companies. Ayahuasca tourism is a growing industry. Serious assaults, robberies and deaths have occurred. Thoroughly research Ayahuasca tour operators before you book. Be alert to the potential risks around drink spiking and methanol poisoning through consuming alcoholic drinks. See our advice on partying safely while overseas. Peru can experience natural disasters and severe weather. To protect yourself in a natural disaster, keep your passport in a secure, waterproof location, monitor local media and follow the advice of local authorities. Travellers using unlicensed taxis have been victims of robbery, assault and rape. Don't hail taxis from the street. Use a phone dispatch service or taxi service app to book a licensed taxi. Criminals target cars stopped at traffic lights. Keep your doors and windows locked, even when moving. Members of a local terrorist group may still be active in remote areas, particularly the Southern Highlands. Take care when travelling outside of populated regions. Full travel advice: Safety

themes.heading: Health
themes.text: Many parts of Peru are at high altitudes. You can develop altitude sickness above 2500m. If you plan to travel to these areas, consult your doctor before leaving. Ensure your travel insurance covers emergency evacuation from altitude and related medical costs. Yellow fever is a risk in Peru. Get vaccinated before you travel. You will need a yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter some countries after visiting Peru. Peru is currently experiencing a dengue outbreak. To protect yourself from mosquito-borne diseases, make sure your accommodation is insect-proof, use insect repellent and wear long, loose, light-coloured clothing. Consult your doctor before travel for advice on prevention and get advice if you become ill. Zika virus is common in jungle regions. If you're pregnant, discuss your travel plans with your doctor before you leave. Malaria is also a risk in Peru. Consult your doctor about how to prevent malaria. Other infectious diseases include cholera, hepatitis, tuberculosis, typhoid, leishmaniasis and rabies. Drink boiled or bottled water. Avoid raw or undercooked food. If an animal bites or scratches you, get immediate medical help. Full travel advice: Health

themes.heading: Local laws
themes.text: Don't use or carry illegal drugs. Penalties for drug offences are severe and include lengthy prison sentences. Officials use up-to-date technology to detect drugs. You must carry photo identification at all times. Be careful when taking photos. It's illegal to photograph infrastructure and military or police sites and personnel. If you're unsure, and local authorities are present, ask them before taking a photograph. Always behave respectfully. Indecent behaviour, including not showing respect at cultural, historical or sacred sites, is against the law. Authorities have detained Australians for this. It's illegal to export antiques and artefacts from pre-colonial Peru. If you want to buy and export a reproduction, use a reputable dealer who can provide the right documents. Dual nationals aged under 18 must travel with both of their passports. Children travelling with one parent or unaccompanied children must carry a notarial permit ('permiso notarial') from the non-travelling parent(s) to depart Peru. This applies if they have resident status in Peru or have stayed in Peru for over 183 days in one year. Full travel advice: Local laws

themes.heading: Travel
themes.text: Tourists don't need a visa. You can get a permit to stay for up to 90 days when you arrive. If you overstay your permit, you'll have to pay a fine before leaving the country. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Peru for the latest details. Follow the advice of local authorities, as restrictions may change at short notice. Emergency passports can be used to enter, transit or depart Peru, as long as it has at least 6 months validity. Ensure you meet all current entry requirements when entering Ecuador. Full travel advice: Travel

themes.heading: Local contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. Contact the Australian Embassy in Lima for consular assistance. To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy’s social media accounts. Full travel advice: Local contacts

themes.heading: States of Emergency
themes.text: A state of emergency is in effect for Lima and Callao. There is an increased security presence, with armed forces on the streets in both areas. The Peruvian government frequently declare states of emergency in various regions or cities including Lima, in response to civil unrest, crime, health concerns, or natural disasters. These declarations can be made at any time and without prior warning. During a state of emergency, Peru's armed forces may assist the police in maintaining law and order. This may include restricting movement, implementing curfews, conducting searches and detaining individuals. In a state of emergency, check media for updates and follow the instructions of local authorities Information on states of emergency is published in the legal gazette El Peruano official newspaper (in Spanish).

themes.heading: Demonstrations and Protests
themes.text: Demonstrations and protests occur frequently in Peru. These can cause some disruption to travel services throughout the country and sometimes turn violent. These include airport and land border closures, railways, roads, river blockades and suspension of transport services to and from isolated tourist destinations like Machu Picchu. In Lima, the historic centre is often the site of demonstrations. If you plan to travel by road, research your planned route carefully, including regularly checking the official list of road closures (in Spanish), and take precautions to ensure your safety. National or regional strikes can be called at short notice, further disrupting domestic air travel, public transport and road networks. To protect yourself during periods of unrest: monitor the media for updates avoid areas affected by demonstrations and protests follow the advice of local authorities make sure you travel with enough food, water, local currency and personal medication. Contact your airline or tour operator to confirm arrangements before you travel. If you're near a demonstration, leave if it's safe to do so. It's illegal for foreigners in Peru to participate in political activities, including demonstrations against the government. You may face detention or deportation if you take part in a demonstration. More information: Demonstrations and civil unrest

themes.heading: Crime
themes.text: Violent crime is common in Peru, including in the cities of: Lima Cusco Arequipa Iquitos Violent crimes include: sexual assault armed robbery and muggings piracy You could encounter: armed robbery, assault and piracy on Amazon River boats theft as you sleep on intercity bus routes between Lima, Ica, Nazca and Cusco assault and robbery at gunpoint on intercity buses bogus roadblocks or checkpoints on roads outside major cities after dark If you're sexually assaulted and decide to report it to the police, do it as soon as you can. You can expect to be examined to obtain forensic evidence as part of the investigation. If you delay reporting, you may experience more scrutiny by local authorities and some evidence may be lost. More information: Advice for women travellers

themes.heading: Taxis
themes.text: Travellers using unlicensed taxis have been victims of robbery, assault and rape. Use a phone dispatch service or taxi service app to book a licensed taxi. Ask for help from staff at hotels, hostels, restaurants or entertainment venues. Be careful and pay attention to suspicious behaviour, even when taking transport booked via apps. If possible, avoid taking taxis or ride-shares by yourself.

themes.heading: Petty crime
themes.text: Petty crime, such as pickpocketing and bag snatching, is common. Thieves are often well dressed. Criminals target people walking alone after dark, especially leaving bars or nightclubs. Thieves frequently target mobile phones. Be aware of your surroundings before using your mobile phone in public spaces and be discreet while using it. Avoid using your phone curb-side on the street, as you may be targeted by snatch-and-grab thieves on motorcycles. Hotspots for thieves include: public areas hotels conference centres restaurants Smash-and-grab attacks are common in various locations around Lima and other cities. Thieves snatch items from cars stopped at traffic lights. If you plan to go on a cruise, check the company has adequate security before booking.

themes.heading: Personal security
themes.text: Travellers in Peru can be victims of: food or drink spiking, followed by robbery or assault 'express kidnappings', where kidnappers force victims to withdraw money from ATMs before releasing them To protect yourself from crime: don't accept drinks, food, gum or cigarettes from strangers or people you have just met don't leave food or drink unattended exchange money in banks, exchange bureaus or in your hotel use ATMs in banks, shopping centres or hotels where possible

themes.heading: Ayahuasca tourism
themes.text: Ayahuasca tourism is a growing industry in the jungle regions. Shamans perform psychedelic rituals of spiritual cleansing. Ayahuasca is not illegal, but some participants have been assaulted, including sexual assault, and robbed. Ceremonies often take place in remote areas with no access to medical or mental health resources and limited communication with local authorities. Most facilities lack basic first aid or emergency plans for people who suffer physical or mental effects after ceremonies. Participants report symptoms from being more alert but out of control through to amnesia. Several tourists have died after consuming the hallucinogenic substances in these ceremonies. If you decide to take part in ayahuasca tourism: research potential ayahuasca tour operators before signing up avoid participating in ayahuasca rituals without a trusted friend present The Peruvian Ministry of Health has produced a guide (in Spanish) on the safe and informed use of ayahuasca.

themes.heading: Border areas and VRAEM
themes.text: Drug traffickers operate in: the border area between Peru and Colombia the valley of the Apurímac, Ene and Mantaro Rivers (VRAEM region) Take additional precautions in these areas. Reconsider your need to travel to the Valley of the Apurimac, Ene and Mantaro Rivers (VRAEM). Cocaine production and trafficking occurs in the VRAEM. Travel is particularly dangerous in areas where there is coca cultivation and processing. Reconsider your need to travel within 20km of the border with Colombia, and areas bordering Ecuador in the regions of Loreto, Amazonas and Cajamarca. Armed guerrilla forces from Colombia sometimes enter Peru’s remote areas.

themes.heading: Kidnapping
themes.text: Kidnapping can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe. Foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped overseas whilst travelling. Kidnapping occurs in Peru, including in Lima, and is primarily perpetrated by criminal groups. Tourists travelling alone are particularly at risk, including of ‘express kidnapping’. Victims may be abducted for a short period and forced to withdraw money from ATMs for their release. Criminals may pose as taxi drivers. Foreigners and foreign interests, including mining operations, are seen as lucrative targets. Pirates have targeted tourist vessels for kidnapping and / or robbery in the Amazon River. Virtual kidnapping scams targeting family and friends of travellers, also occur in Peru. If, despite our advice, you travel to an area with a high risk of kidnapping, our ability to provide assistance in these destinations is extremely limited. You’re responsible for your own safety. You can find more information on how to reduce your risk of kidnapping here: Kidnapping The Australian Government's longstanding policy is that it doesn't make payments or concessions to kidnappers. Ransom payments to kidnappers have funded further terrorist attacks and criminal activity. Ransom payments to terrorist groups are likely to contravene Australian counter-terrorism financing laws. More information: Scams that affect travellers Reducing the risk of piracy

themes.heading: Terrorism
themes.text: Members of a local terrorist group may still be in isolated areas throughout Peru, especially in the Central and Southern Highlands. This includes the Valley of the Apurimac, Ene and Mantaro Rivers (VRAEM) where drug traffickers operate. Take care if you travel to: San Martin Huanuco Pasco Junin Ucayali Huancavelica Ayacucho Apurimac These places may harbour members of the Shining Path terrorist movement, and incidents of domestic terrorism have occurred in the region. To protect yourself from terrorism: be alert to possible threats, especially in the Southern Highlands monitor the media for updates take official warnings seriously follow the advice of local authorities report any suspicious activity or items to the police If there's an attack, leave the area as soon as it's safe. Avoid areas affected in case of secondary attacks. Terrorism is a threat worldwide. More information: Terrorism

themes.heading: Tours and adventure activities
themes.text: Australians have died from injuries sustained in adventure travel accidents in Peru. Ziplining, rafting, diving, sand-dune buggy-riding and other adventure tour operators are not always regulated and don't always follow safety and maintenance standards. The Inca Trail closes temporarily mostly in February, every year for maintenance. Some companies will still operate. Heavy rainfall especially during the rainy season (November to May) can make parts of the trail impassable and dangerous. If you plan to do an adventure activity: check if your travel insurance policy covers it ask about and insist on minimum safety requirements always use available safety gear, such as life jackets or seatbelts If proper safety equipment isn't available, use another provider. To reduce your risks: seek advice from local authorities adjust your plans if the weather makes conditions unsafe monitor weather conditions use an experienced guide on the Inca Trail or other treks

themes.heading: Tourism assistance or complaints
themes.text: Contact your provider with any complaints about tourist services or products. Phone iPeru: +51 1 574-8000 (tourist assistance service with English-speaking personnel)

themes.heading: Extreme weather and natural disasters
themes.text: Peru can experience natural disasters and severe weather, such as: flooding landslides earthquakes tsunamis volcanic eruptions bushfires To protect yourself if a natural disaster is approaching: keep your passport in a secure, waterproof location monitor local media and follow the advice of local authorities keep in contact with friends and family contact your tour operator or airline More information: Global Disaster Alert and Coordination System

themes.heading: Flooding and landslides
themes.text: Peru has a variety of climates. The rainy season is from November to May. Flooding and landslides are common in the Andes during this period. Rail, road and air services may be disrupted. Heavy rain can cause flooding and landslides especially in the Andes mountain range, affecting tourist areas like: Machu Picchu the Inca Trail Aguas Calientes (Machu Picchu town) This can result in travel delays.

themes.heading: Earthquakes and tsunamis
themes.text: Peru is in an active earthquake zone. Earthquakes and tsunamis can occur. A tsunami can arrive very soon after a nearby tremor or earthquake. Be alert to warnings. If you're near the coast, move immediately to high ground if advised by local authorities or if you: feel a strong earthquake that makes it hard to stand up feel a weak, rolling earthquake that lasts a minute or more see a sudden rise or fall in sea level hear loud and unusual noises from the sea Don't wait for official warnings, such as alarms or sirens. Once on high ground, monitor local media. More information: U.S Tsunami Warning Centers (United States government) Geophysical Institute of Peru (in Spanish) Hydrography and Navigation Directorate of Peru (in Spanish)

themes.heading: Volcanoes
themes.text: Several volcanoes in southern Peru are active. Ubinas volcano in the Moquegua region and Sabancaya volcano in the Arequipa region have erupted multiple times. Eruptions can occur at any time and without warning. Exposure to volcanic ash, dust and toxic fumes can harm your health, especially if you have existing respiratory problems. To protect yourself if there's an eruption: stay inside with windows and doors shut put damp towels at door thresholds and other draft sources if ash is falling monitor local media for advice on possible risks If you need to go outside, avoid contact with ash. Wear a disposable face mask and change it frequently. Wear long clothing and goggles. Seek local advice on recent volcanic activity before hiking or trekking near active volcanoes. More information: Geophysical Institute of Peru Instituto Geofisico del Peru (IGP) (in Spanish) Geology, Mineralogy and Metallurgy Institute Instituto Geologico Minero y Metalurigico (INGEMMET) (in Spanish)

themes.heading: Travel insurance
themes.text: Get comprehensive travel insurance before you leave and closely read the Product Disclosure Statement (PDS) for what is and isn’t covered. It must cover all medical costs overseas, including evacuation. The Australian Government will not pay for these costs. If you can’t afford travel insurance, you shouldn’t travel. This is true for everyone, even if you are healthy and fit. If you don’t have insurance, you might have to pay thousands of dollars up front for medical care. Before you travel, check your policy, make sure: you know what activities and medical care your insurance covers, including evacuations your insurance covers you for your whole trip, including any stopovers you know any exclusions – for example, you might need to pay extra for activities like motorcycling, snow sports, scuba diving or bungee jumping. More information: Travel insurance

themes.heading: Physical and mental health
themes.text: Travelling overseas can be stressful and uncertain. You may experience unfamiliar environments, travel disruptions, changes in normal routines and limited access to health care. Not taking your prescribed medications is a common cause of mental ill-health for Australians overseas. If you are concerned about your welfare, or someone else's, contact your nearest Australian embassy, high commission, or consulate. You can also call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305. Mental health resources: Information and advice on mental illness (HealthDirect). Living with mental illness (BeyondBlue) Medicare Mental Health (the Department of Health and Aged Care) More information: General health advice Mental health support overseas

themes.heading: Altitude sickness
themes.text: You're at risk of altitude sickness if you travel above 2500m. The risk is greater if your ascent is rapid. Altitude sickness can be life-threatening and can affect anyone, even if you're healthy. You're more at risk of altitude sickness if you: have had altitude sickness before exercise or drink alcohol before you get used to the altitude have health problems that affect breathing Many areas of Peru are above 2500m, including: Cusco Colca Canyon Puno and Lake Titicaca See your doctor for specific advice. Check if your insurance covers emergency evacuation from altitude and related medical costs.

themes.heading: Insect-borne diseases
themes.text: Yellow fever is widespread in Peru. Yellow fever is a potentially fatal virus spread by mosquitoes. It's prevented by vaccination. Get vaccinated before you travel. Peru is currently experiencing a dengue outbreak. Monitor local media for up-to-date advice on risk levels in particular areas. Zika and oropouche are a risk. If you're pregnant, the Australian Department of Health recommends that you: discuss any travel plans with your doctor consider deferring non-essential travel to affected areas Malaria is also a risk in Peru. To protect yourself from disease: make sure your accommodation is insect-proof use insect repellent wear long, loose, light-coloured clothing Consult your doctor about how to prevent malaria. Get medical advice if you have a fever, muscle pain, rash or severe headache.

themes.heading: Other health risks
themes.text: Waterborne, foodborne, parasitic, and other infectious diseases are common. These include: cholera typhoid hepatitis tuberculosis rabies leishmaniasis Severe outbreaks sometimes occur. To protect yourself from illness: drink boiled water or bottled water with sealed lids avoid ice cubes avoid raw and undercooked food, such as salads avoid contact with dogs and other mammals More information: Infectious diseases

themes.heading: Medical care
themes.text: Medical facilities are adequate in major cities but limited elsewhere. Doctors and hospitals often require payment before they will treat you, including for emergency care. If you become seriously ill or injured, you may need to be evacuated to a place with suitable facilities. Medical evacuation can be very expensive.

themes.heading: Travelling with children
themes.text: Children under 18 years old travelling on an Australian passport must obtain written permission (Autorización de Viaje Notarial) from the non-travelling parent(s) to leave the country. This applies if they have resident status or have stayed in Peru for over 183 days in one year. For more information, see the Peruvian government's website.

themes.heading: Drugs
themes.text: Penalties for drug offences are severe. They include lengthy prison sentences. Prison conditions in Peru are challenging. Don't carry or use illegal drugs. Trained staff use technology to detect illegal drugs at Lima's International Airport and throughout Peru. Australians have been jailed for long periods for drug offences.

themes.heading: Visas and border measures
themes.text: Each country or territory decides its own entry and exit rules, including health border screening measures. To find out what you need to enter a destination, ask their embassy, consulate or immigration office.

themes.heading: Visas
themes.text: Australian tourists don't need a visa. You can get a permit to stay for up to 90 days when you arrive. The maximum stay permitted is 183 days in one year. If you overstay your permit, you'll have to pay a fine before leaving the country. In other situations, you'll need to apply for a visa through an embassy or consulate of Peru. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. You can contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Peru for the latest details. They'll tell you about visas, currency, customs and other travel requirements.

themes.heading: Border measures
themes.text: International airports in Peru will not issue immigration entry or departure stamps. Only digital records will be kept of entry and exit from the country by air. You can check the number of days you have been granted to stay legally in Peru, on the Peruvian Immigration Office website (in Spanish). If you enter Peru from Bolivia either by walking or by bus or taxi, you must make sure your passport is stamped with a Peruvian entry stamp at the immigration office in Desaguadero or Copacabana (Puno region). You'll need to go to the immigration checkpoint, as they won't seek you out. If you enter Peru overland from Ecuador, you must make sure your passport is stamped with a Peruvian entry stamp at the local immigration office. You may need to ask for directions to the immigration office. Most people crossing the border with Ecuador enter Peru through Aguas Verdes (Tumbes region). If your passport is not stamped at the border with Ecuador, you can have it stamped at the Immigration Office in the city of Tumbes. If you haven't arranged an entry stamp to evidence your entry at land borders or seaports, you'll need to apply for an exit or expulsion order at the Immigration Office in Lima. You won't be allowed to leave Peru without this, and these orders may prevent you from re-entering Peru for up to 10 years. Only cross the border at official checkpoints. Ensure you also get an exit stamp from the country you're departing.

themes.heading: Travel via the United States
themes.text: If you're travelling through the US, you must meet US entry and transit requirements. Check your visa requirements with a US embassy or consulate well in advance of your travel. More information: Travel advice for the US

themes.heading: Travel to Ecuador
themes.text: If you're entering Ecuador, ensure you meet all current entry requirements. More information: Travel advice for Ecuador

themes.heading: Yellow fever vaccination
themes.text: You may need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Peru. Some airlines may want to see one when you leave. You will need a yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter some countries after visiting Peru. Check entry requirements before travelling. If you've visited Peru in the previous 6 days, you'll need a valid certificate to enter Australia. Find out about returning to Australia after exposure to yellow fever.

themes.heading: Passport
themes.text: You need to have at least 6 months validity remaining in your passport to enter Peru. Emergency travel documents can be used to enter, transit, or depart Peru as long as they have at least 6 months of validity from the moment of entry in Peru.

themes.heading: Lost or stolen passport
themes.text: Many countries require your passport to be valid for at least 6 months before entering or departing. This includes for transit. If your passport expires too soon, you might not be able to travel. The Australian Government doesn’t make these rules. Check your passport’s expiry date before you go. If you’re not sure, it’s a good idea to get a new passport. If you leave Peru with a replacement passport different from the passport you entered Peru (e.g., an emergency passport), you'll need to show a Police report for the loss of the previous passport to the Immigration officers at the moment of departure.

themes.heading: Passport with ‘X’ gender identifier
themes.text: Australian passports comply with international standards for sex and gender. If your passport has an 'X' in the sex field, we can’t guarantee your entry or transit to other countries. Ask the embassy or consulate of your destinations if they’ll accept your passport with an 'X' gender marker. Check all transit destinations too. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Money
themes.text: The local currency is the Peruvian Nuevo Sol (PEN). Declare all amounts more than of $US10,000 in any currency on arrival. This covers all forms of currency, not only cash. The maximum amount permitted is $US 30,000 or equivalent. ATM facilities are widely available. Credit cards are usually accepted. Beware of counterfeit currency scams from unofficial money changers.

themes.heading: Landmines
themes.text: Landmines are being removed but remain a threat in some regions, including: Loreto Amazonas (Cordillera del Condor) Cajamarca Tacna Tumbes Cross land borders at official checkpoints only. Cross borders at official checkpoints only.

themes.heading: Driving permit
themes.text: You can use your Australian driver's license to drive in Peru for the duration of a tourist visa (maximum 183 days in one year). If you're staying in Peru longer, you'll need an International Driving Permit (IDP).

themes.heading: Road travel
themes.text: You're more likely to be killed in a motor vehicle accident in Peru than in Australia. Driving hazards include: poorly maintained roads and vehicles aggressive local driving practices poor road lighting Fatal traffic accidents are common and often involve intercity buses. Travelling by road outside major cities after dark is dangerous due to the risk of criminal activity. This includes bogus roadblocks or checkpoints. If you plan to drive: check you have adequate insurance cover learn local traffic laws and practices More information: Road safety

themes.heading: Motorcycles
themes.text: Your travel insurance policy may not cover you when riding a motorbike, quad bike or similar vehicle. Always wear a helmet.

themes.heading: Taxis
themes.text: Travellers using unlicensed taxis have been victims of robbery, assault and rape. To stay safe when you arrive in Peru, either: arrange a taxi at the counter in Lima's international airport use your hotel transfer service book a reputable transfer service To protect yourself if you're travelling by taxi: don't hail taxis from the street book through an app-based service ask the staff at hotels, hostels, restaurants or places of entertainment to book a licensed taxi

themes.heading: Public transport
themes.text: Intercity buses can be involved in road accidents. They can also be targeted by criminals. Use a reputable transport or bus company to reduce risks. More information: Getting around safetly

themes.heading: Trains
themes.text: Demonstrations, strikes and derailments can disrupt train travel, including those operating between Arequipa-Cusco-Puno and Cusco-Ollantaytambo-Machu Picchu.

themes.heading: Air travel
themes.text: DFAT doesn’t give advice about how safe different airlines or flight paths are. Safety risks and weather events may cause flight delays and cancellations. Contact your travel agent or airline to confirm your plans. Airlines and airports can restrict what you can take in your luggage. Regulations may be different to those in Australia. Check with your airline before you travel. Light aircraft and helicopter flights may be hazardous due to a variety of conditions. These include changeable weather and harsh geography. Before you book a scenic flight over the Nazca Lines, check the airline company: is licensed has a good safety record Check Peru's air safety profile with the Aviation Safety Network. More information: Air travel

themes.heading: Consular contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.

themes.heading: Australian Embassy, Lima
themes.text: Avenida La Paz 1049, 10th Floor Miraflores, Lima, 18, Peru Phone: +51 1 630 0500 Email: lima.passports@dfat.gov.au Website: https://peru.embassy.gov.au/lima/home.html Facebook: Australia en Perú y Bolivia X: x.com/embauslima Instagram: @embauslima Australia has a Consulate headed by an Honorary Consul in Cusco. The Consulate provides limited consular assistance. It does not provide visa and immigration services, notarial services or issue passports. For full consular services, contact the Australian Embassy in Lima.

themes.heading: 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre
themes.text: In a consular emergency, if you can't contact an embassy, call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on: • +61 2 6261 3305 from overseas • 1300 555 135 in Australia
